original_brief.headline: AB1751 Strips Cities of All Impact Fee Authority
original_brief.subtext: Water, sewer, and traffic costs shift to existing taxpayers under this bill
original_brief.typographic_element: Bill number AB1751 as oversized display type, upper-left corner, in warm amber
original_brief.optional_graphic: Faint outline of a row of townhomes, minimal flat style, anchored to the bottom-right corner
original_brief.ai_image_prompt: A minimal flat design graphic on a deep forest green background. A row of simple geometric townhouse silhouettes lines the bottom edge, rendered as clean outlines in warm gold. The overall aesthetic is calm, policy-focused, and authoritative — a professional advocacy graphic with no decorative clutter. No text. No typography. No people. No logos.

briefs.reasoning: The Bill Spotlight needs to feel like a breaking policy brief landing on a city manager's desk — authoritative, data-forward, and slightly confrontational in its directness. The bill number AB1751 in oversized warm amber is the visual hook that stops the scroll; everything else subordinates to it. This post differentiates from the Action Alert (which will carry urgency through headline dominance) and the Mission Frame (which will use open space for gravitas) by being the tightest, most left-anchored, most typographically compressed of the three — the layout itself communicates 'read this now, there is a specific thing happening.' The faint townhome outline at bottom-right adds spatial grounding without softening the alarm register.
briefs.square_prompt: Create a 1:1 square social media graphic (1080x1080px) for the City Managers Association of California. This is a bill spotlight policy advocacy graphic — institutional, clean, authoritative, with an alarm-and-authority emotional register. No photos. No people. No logos. No gradients. Background: flat solid deep forest green (#1a3d2b) filling the entire frame. Compositional structure: Left-anchored typographic layout with strong vertical stacking. The frame is dense and purposeful — negative space is minimized to reinforce the breaking-news urgency of a legislative alert. Element placement and hierarchy — FIRST: The bill number 'AB1751' renders as oversized display type in the upper-left quadrant, set in a bold sans-serif typeface. This text is warm amber (#c8941a) and dramatically large — occupying roughly 35–40% of the frame's vertical height, with the 'AB' slightly smaller than '1751' to create a two-beat typographic punch. The text aligns flush to the left margin with generous top padding. This is the dominant anchor and must be unmistakably the largest element. SECOND: Immediately below 'AB1751', with tight vertical spacing that signals urgency, render the headline text 'AB1751 Strips Cities of All Impact Fee Authority' in white (#ffffff), bold weight, flush-left, set at approximately 40–45% the height of the AB1751 display type. This is the secondary typographic element — large, commanding, but clearly subordinate to the bill number above. Line breaks should fall naturally at 'Cities of All' and 'Impact Fee Authority' to create a three-line block. THIRD: A warm amber (#c8941a) horizontal rule or thin accent stripe, approximately 2–3px tall, spanning roughly 80% of the frame width from the left edge, sits as a visual separator between the headline and the subtext below. This stripe is structural — it divides the alarm statement from the explanatory line. FOURTH: Below the amber stripe, render the subtext 'Water, sewer, and traffic costs shift to existing taxpayers under this bill' in white (#ffffff), regular weight, flush-left, at approximately 50–55% the size of the headline text. This is the explanatory tier — smaller, lighter weight, clearly tertiary in the reading order. FIFTH: In the bottom-right corner of the frame, a faint outline illustration of a row of townhomes in minimal flat style — rendered as a single-weight line drawing in white at approximately 10–12% opacity. The townhomes are simple and silhouetted, anchored to the bottom edge, visible as a ghost element that grounds the policy content in physical urban reality without competing with the typography. The illustration occupies roughly the bottom-right 30% of the frame. Style constraints: No photographs. No human figures. No organizational logos. No gradients. No drop shadows. All text must be exactly as written — no paraphrasing, no abbreviation.
briefs.landscape_prompt: Create a 16:9 landscape social media graphic (1920x1080px) for the City Managers Association of California. This is a bill spotlight policy advocacy graphic — institutional, clean, authoritative, with an alarm-and-authority emotional register. No photos. No people. No logos. No gradients. Background: flat solid deep forest green (#1a3d2b) filling the entire frame. Compositional structure: The horizontal expanse is divided into two asymmetric zones. The LEFT ZONE occupies approximately 60% of the frame width — this is the typographic anchor zone, dense and left-aligned, carrying all text content. The RIGHT ZONE occupies the remaining 40% — it holds the faint townhome illustration at bottom-right and otherwise breathes, reinforcing that the left zone is the content authority while the right zone provides visual resolution. This split differentiates the landscape from the square by using horizontal tension rather than vertical stacking — the bill number and headline feel like they are pushing rightward with legislative force. Element placement and hierarchy — FIRST: In the upper-left of the left zone, the bill number 'AB1751' renders as oversized display type in warm amber (#c8941a), bold sans-serif, dramatically large — approximately 30–35% of the total frame height. 'AB' is slightly smaller in tracking than '1751', creating a visual two-beat. The text is flush-left with comfortable left-edge padding. This is the unmistakable dominant anchor of the entire composition. SECOND: Directly below 'AB1751', with compressed vertical spacing that signals urgency, the headline 'AB1751 Strips Cities of All Impact Fee Authority' renders in white (#ffffff), bold weight, flush-left, at approximately 38–42% the height of the bill number display type. In landscape format, the headline may run across up to two lines — allow natural breaks at 'All Impact Fee Authority' if needed. The headline should feel large and authoritative in its zone. THIRD: A warm amber (#c8941a) horizontal accent stripe, 2–3px tall, spans from the left edge across the full width of the left zone — approximately 60% of the total frame width. This stripe separates the alarm statement above from the explanatory subtext below, functioning as a structural rule that organizes the reading sequence. FOURTH: Below the amber stripe, the subtext 'Water, sewer, and traffic costs shift to existing taxpayers under this bill' renders in white (#ffffff), regular weight, flush-left, at approximately 50% the size of the headline. This is the explanatory tier — clearly tertiary, lighter in weight, supporting the headline rather than competing with it. FIFTH: In the far bottom-right corner of the right zone, a faint outline illustration of a row of townhomes in minimal flat style — single-weight line drawing in white at approximately 10–12% opacity. The townhomes are simple, silhouetted, and anchored to the bottom-right edge of the frame. In the landscape format, the townhomes have more horizontal room and can extend slightly wider than in the square version, filling the bottom-right approximately 28–30% of the total frame. The illustration is a ghost element — present but subordinate, grounding the legislative content in the physical urban environment it governs. Style constraints: No photographs. No human figures. No organizational logos. No gradients. No drop shadows. All text must be exactly as written — no paraphrasing, no abbreviation.

original_brief.headline: AB2288 Forces Residential Use Into Industrial Zones
original_brief.subtext: Cities cannot require developers to fund water, sewer, or park upgrades
original_brief.typographic_element: Bill number AB2288 as oversized display type, upper-left corner, in warm amber
original_brief.optional_graphic: Split-field abstract graphic: industrial building silhouette on left transitioning to residential building silhouette on right, flat minimal style, bottom of frame
original_brief.ai_image_prompt: A bold flat graphic on a deep forest green background. On the left side of the lower frame, a simple geometric industrial warehouse silhouette in warm gold outline; on the right, a simple residential building silhouette in white outline. The two shapes meet in the center with a subtle directional arrow element suggesting forced transition. Clean, minimal, policy-advocacy aesthetic. No text. No typography. No people. No logos.

briefs.reasoning: An Action Alert demands kinetic urgency — the composition must feel like something is happening right now that requires immediate response. To differentiate from Post 1's centered authoritative alarm and Post 3's open resolute negative space, this post uses asymmetric tension: the oversized AB2288 in warm amber anchors hard upper-left, creating a forward-leaning diagonal energy that pulls the eye rightward and downward through the headline and subtext. The warm amber is deployed as both the dominant typographic element (bill number) AND a bold diagonal accent slash cutting across the frame — not a safe horizontal stripe — reinforcing kinetic motion. The split-field industrial-to-residential silhouette at the bottom grounds the operational reality while the tight compressed layout above it communicates imminence.
briefs.square_prompt:
Create a 1:1 square format social media policy advocacy graphic for the City Managers Association of California. This is an Action Alert post designed to communicate urgency and compel immediate professional response.

Background: flat solid deep forest green (#1a3d2b). No gradients, no textures, no photographic elements.

COMPOSITION — ASYMMETRIC TENSION LAYOUT:
The layout uses deliberate asymmetric tension to communicate urgency. The bill number anchors hard to the upper-left. A bold warm amber diagonal accent slash runs from upper-left to mid-right across the upper third of the frame, cutting behind the bill number, creating kinetic forward motion. The headline and subtext stack tightly in the left-center zone. The silhouette element runs across the bottom.

TYPOGRAPHIC HIERARCHY:
1. DOMINANT: 'AB2288' — oversized display type, positioned in the upper-left corner, approximately 22% of frame height tall, warm amber (#c8941a), bold sans-serif, all caps. This is the first thing the eye lands on.
2. SECONDARY: 'AB2288 Forces Residential Use Into Industrial Zones' — white (#ffffff), bold sans-serif, approximately 8% of frame height, left-aligned, positioned in the center-left zone below the bill number. This is the action message.
3. TERTIARY: 'Cities cannot require developers to fund water, sewer, or park upgrades' — white (#ffffff), regular weight sans-serif, approximately 5% of frame height, left-aligned, positioned tightly below the headline with minimal leading.

ACCENT ELEMENT:
A bold warm amber (#c8941a) diagonal slash accent — a parallelogram or sharp diagonal band, not a horizontal stripe — cuts across the upper-left quadrant of the frame, running roughly from the upper-left corner toward the right edge at approximately 20% from the top. This slash sits behind the 'AB2288' bill number text and creates the kinetic diagonal energy that differentiates this post from a static informational layout. The slash is a flat geometric shape, not a line.

OPTIONAL GRAPHIC ELEMENT:
At the bottom of the frame, a flat minimal split-field silhouette illustration in a muted, slightly lighter forest green (suggesting depth without gradients): on the left side, a simplified industrial building silhouette — flat roof, rectangular massing, suggesting warehouse or manufacturing — and on the right side, a simplified residential building silhouette — pitched roof, smaller scale, suggesting housing. The two silhouettes are directly adjacent, the transition between them representing the forced land use conversion described in the bill. These silhouettes occupy approximately the bottom 18% of the frame. They are flat, minimal, single-color shapes — slightly lighter than the background green — with no detail lines, no windows, no realistic rendering.

SPACING:
The composition is tight and compressed — minimal breathing room between typographic elements — reinforcing urgency. Left margin is tight, approximately 6% of frame width. There is no centered layout. Everything leans left and forward.

STYLE CONSTRAINTS: No photographs. No people. No logos. No gradients anywhere. No decorative flourishes. No realistic illustration. This is a flat, institutional policy advocacy graphic. All text must be rendered exactly as specified — no paraphrasing, no abbreviation.
briefs.landscape_prompt:
Create a 16:9 landscape format social media policy advocacy graphic for the City Managers Association of California. This is an Action Alert post designed to communicate urgency and compel immediate professional response.

Background: flat solid deep forest green (#1a3d2b). No gradients, no textures, no photographic elements.

COMPOSITION — HORIZONTAL SPLIT-ZONE WITH DIAGONAL ENERGY:
The 16:9 horizontal expanse is divided into two functional zones. The LEFT ZONE (approximately 55% of frame width) contains all typography, anchored left with tight compressed stacking. The RIGHT ZONE (approximately 45% of frame width) contains breathing room and the silhouette graphic element at bottom-right, allowing the typography to breathe rightward visually without crowding. A bold warm amber diagonal accent slash cuts from the upper-left corner across the entire upper band of the frame, extending to approximately 65% of the frame width before terminating — this diagonal thrust creates strong horizontal kinetic energy that moves the eye from left to right across the format, exploiting the landscape proportion.

TYPOGRAPHIC HIERARCHY:
1. DOMINANT: 'AB2288' — oversized display type anchored to the upper-left corner, approximately 28% of frame height tall, warm amber (#c8941a), bold sans-serif, all caps. The bill number is dramatically larger than any other text element. It is the first element the eye encounters and should fill a commanding portion of the upper-left quadrant.
2. SECONDARY: 'AB2288 Forces Residential Use Into Industrial Zones' — white (#ffffff), bold sans-serif, approximately 9% of frame height, left-aligned, positioned in the center-left zone. Line breaks naturally for readability — 'AB2288 Forces Residential Use' on line one, 'Into Industrial Zones' on line two if needed.
3. TERTIARY: 'Cities cannot require developers to fund water, sewer, or park upgrades' — white (#ffffff), regular weight sans-serif, approximately 5.5% of frame height, left-aligned, positioned tightly below the headline. Tight leading reinforces urgency.

ACCENT ELEMENT:
A bold warm amber (#c8941a) diagonal slash accent — a wide parallelogram or sharp diagonal band — originates at the upper-left corner and sweeps rightward across approximately 60% of the frame width, positioned in the upper 22% of the frame. This slash sits behind the 'AB2288' bill number text, partially visible to either side of the letters. It is a flat geometric shape creating strong horizontal diagonal thrust that feels like motion, not decoration. The slash is the structural differentiator from the other posts in this set.

OPTIONAL GRAPHIC ELEMENT:
In the lower-right zone of the frame (occupying the bottom 22% of the right 45% of the frame), a flat minimal split-field silhouette illustration: on the left side of the element, a simplified industrial building silhouette — flat roof, rectangular massing, suggesting warehouse or manufacturing — and directly adjacent on the right, a simplified residential building silhouette — pitched roof, smaller scale, suggesting single-family or multifamily housing. The silhouettes are rendered in a muted tone slightly lighter than the background green — flat, single-color shapes with no internal detail, no windows, no realistic rendering. The industrial-to-residential transition visualizes the forced land use conversion described in the bill. Positioning the element in the lower-right creates a diagonal visual relationship with the upper-left typographic anchor, reinforcing the kinetic tension of the overall composition.

SPACING:
All typographic elements are left-aligned with a tight left margin of approximately 5% of frame width. Vertical spacing between typographic elements is compressed — minimal leading — reinforcing urgency and forward momentum. The right portion of the frame has intentional open space above the silhouette element, creating contrast between the dense left zone and the open right zone.

STYLE CONSTRAINTS: No photographs. No people. No logos. No gradients anywhere. No decorative flourishes. No realistic illustration. This is a flat, institutional policy advocacy graphic. All text must be rendered exactly as specified — no paraphrasing, no abbreviation.

original_brief.headline: State Mandates the Outcome, Cities Pay the Cost
original_brief.subtext: Three bills this session shift infrastructure costs to local taxpayers
original_brief.typographic_element: The phrase 'AB1751 · AB2288 · SB1014' as a secondary typographic line in warm amber, below the headline
original_brief.optional_graphic: Abstract scale or balance graphic, slightly tilted to suggest imbalance, centered lower third, flat minimal style
original_brief.ai_image_prompt: A minimal flat design graphic on a deep forest green background. In the lower center of the frame, a simple geometric balance scale rendered in warm gold, visibly tilted to one side to suggest imbalance, with clean vector-style lines. The surrounding space is open and uncluttered, conveying institutional weight and professional authority. No text. No typography. No people. No logos.

briefs.reasoning: The Mission Frame needs to feel philosophical and spacious — a declarative truth about the structural relationship between state authority and local fiscal burden, not a data alert. Where Post 1 uses alarm and compressed urgency, and Post 2 uses tight asymmetric tension for agency, this post earns its resolve through open negative space and measured typographic weight. The warm amber accent here should not be a structural stripe (used in the other posts) but rather a delicate typographic thread — the bill numbers as a subordinate amber line beneath the commanding white headline — reinforcing that this post synthesizes multiple specific moments into one larger institutional truth. The abstract scale graphic, slightly tilted, does the conceptual heavy lifting without shouting. This post breathes where the others press forward.
briefs.square_prompt: Create a 1:1 square format policy advocacy graphic for the City Managers Association of California. This is a Mission Frame post — thematic, philosophical, and resolved in tone. It should feel notably more spacious and expansive than a bill spotlight or action alert. Background: flat solid deep forest green (#1a3d2b). No gradients, no textures, no photos, no people, no logos. Compositional structure: The frame is organized in three vertical zones. The upper 55% of the frame holds the typographic composition. The lower 45% holds a subtle abstract graphic element and supporting text. Typography hierarchy, top to bottom: First, a short label line in warm amber (#c8941a), uppercase tracking-wide sans-serif, very small — roughly 6–7% of frame height — reading: 'CITY MANAGERS ASSOCIATION OF CALIFORNIA'. Second, immediately below with a generous but controlled gap, the dominant headline in crisp white (#ffffff), bold condensed sans-serif, commanding and declarative, rendered at approximately 13–15% of frame height per line, centered, spanning no more than 80% of frame width, two lines: 'State Mandates the Outcome,' on the first line and 'Cities Pay the Cost' on the second line. The headline is the gravitational center of the entire composition — it should feel like a statement carved into the frame, not placed on it. Third, directly below the headline with moderate spacing, a single typographic line in warm amber (#c8941a), medium-weight sans-serif, approximately 5–6% of frame height, centered, reading: 'AB1751 · AB2288 · SB1014'. This amber line functions as an anchor thread — it names the specific beneath the universal. Fourth, below the amber bill number line with additional breathing room, a subtext line in white (#ffffff), light-weight sans-serif, approximately 4% of frame height, centered, reading: 'Three bills this session shift infrastructure costs to local taxpayers'. In the lower third of the frame, centered horizontally, render an abstract scale or balance graphic in flat minimal style. The scale has two arms and two pans rendered in clean geometric lines — no photorealism, no shading. The scale is slightly tilted to the right, suggesting imbalance, with the right pan lower than the left. The graphic is rendered in white with warm amber (#c8941a) used only for the central fulcrum point and the lower pan — implying where the cost lands. The scale occupies roughly 25% of frame width and sits comfortably in negative space. No accent stripe across the lower third — the negative space and tilted scale graphic carry the compositional weight here. The overall feeling is institutional resolve: expansive, measured, purposeful.
briefs.landscape_prompt: Create a 16:9 landscape format policy advocacy graphic for the City Managers Association of California. This is a Mission Frame post — thematic, philosophical, and resolved in tone. It should feel notably more spacious and expansive than a bill spotlight or action alert. Background: flat solid deep forest green (#1a3d2b). No gradients, no textures, no photos, no people, no logos. Compositional structure: The landscape format uses a deliberate horizontal split-zone layout. The left two-thirds of the frame is the typographic zone. The right one-third is a breathing zone anchored by the abstract scale graphic. This horizontal division is NOT separated by any line or border — the negative space itself creates the separation. Left zone typography, anchored to the vertical center-left of the frame, stacked top to bottom with generous line spacing: First, a label line in warm amber (#c8941a), uppercase wide-tracked sans-serif, very small — approximately 5% of frame height — reading: 'CITY MANAGERS ASSOCIATION OF CALIFORNIA'. Second, below with a moderate gap, the dominant headline in crisp white (#ffffff), bold condensed sans-serif, rendered at approximately 16–18% of frame height per line, left-aligned to a consistent left margin roughly 8% from the frame edge, two lines: 'State Mandates the Outcome,' on the first line and 'Cities Pay the Cost' on the second line. The headline is the gravitational anchor — it should read as a declarative institutional truth, not a headline placed on a slide. Third, directly below the headline with moderate spacing, a single typographic line in warm amber (#c8941a), medium-weight sans-serif, approximately 5–6% of frame height, left-aligned, reading: 'AB1751 · AB2288 · SB1014'. This amber line names the specific beneath the universal — a grounding thread. Fourth, below the amber bill line with additional spacing, the subtext in white (#ffffff), light-weight sans-serif, approximately 3.5–4% of frame height, left-aligned, reading: 'Three bills this session shift infrastructure costs to local taxpayers'. Right zone: Centered vertically in the right one-third of the frame, render an abstract scale or balance graphic in flat minimal style. The scale has two arms and two pans in clean geometric line work — no photorealism, no shading, no drop shadows. The scale is tilted slightly to the right, with the right pan lower than the left, suggesting imbalance. The graphic is rendered in white with warm amber (#c8941a) used only for the central fulcrum point and the lower-tilted pan, implying where the cost lands. The scale occupies approximately 40% of the right zone's width, floating in open negative space. No horizontal accent stripe — the right-side negative space and the tilted scale carry the resolve and philosophical weight of the composition. The overall feeling is expansive, authoritative, and resolved: a manifesto in form, not a memo.
